questions.question: If △ABC ≅ △PQR, and the perimeter of △ABC is 25 cm, what is the perimeter of △PQR?
questions.explanation: Congruent triangles have identical side lengths, so their perimeters must be equal.

questions.question: In △PQR and △XYZ, PQ = XY, QR = YZ, and ∠Q = ∠Y. Which criterion proves their congruence?
questions.explanation: Two sides and the included angle (the angle between them) satisfy the SAS criterion.

questions.question: In △ABC and △DEF, ∠A = ∠D, ∠B = ∠E, and AB = DE. If BC = 5 cm, then EF must be:
questions.explanation: The triangles are congruent by ASA. By CPCT, corresponding sides BC and EF are equal.

questions.question: Which of the following measurements can form two congruent triangles using the SSS criterion?
questions.explanation: SSS requires all three corresponding sides to be equal in length.

questions.question: In a right-angled △ABC, ∠B = 90° and AC = 10 cm. In right-angled △DEF, ∠E = 90° and DF = 10 cm. What additional info is needed for RHS congruence?
questions.explanation: RHS requires a Right angle, equal Hypotenuses (AC=DF), and one pair of equal sides (BC=EF or AB=DE).

questions.question: If △ABC ≅ △LKM, then side AC corresponds to:
questions.explanation: A is the 1st letter, C is the 3rd. In LKM, 1st is L and 3rd is M. So, AC = LM.

questions.question: Two equilateral triangles are always congruent if:

options.B: One side of one equals one side of the other

questions.explanation: In equilateral triangles, if one side is equal, all sides are equal (SSS). Same area for equilateral triangles also implies same side length.

questions.question: In △ABC, AB = AC and AD is the bisector of ∠A meeting BC at D. △ABD ≅ △ACD by which criterion?
questions.explanation: AB=AC (given), ∠BAD=∠CAD (bisector), and AD=AD (common side).

questions.question: If for two triangles, the SAS criterion fails but all three sides are equal, are they congruent?
questions.explanation: Congruence can be proven by any one of the valid criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS).

questions.question: Given △PQR ≅ △STU. If ∠P = 60° and ∠R = 50°, what is the measure of ∠T?
questions.explanation: ∠Q = 180 - (60+50) = 70°. Since △PQR ≅ △STU, ∠T corresponds to ∠Q, so ∠T = 70°.

questions.question: In △ABC and △PQR, AB = PQ, BC = QR and ∠C = ∠R. Are the triangles necessarily congruent?

options.B: No, because the angle is not included

questions.explanation: SAS requires the angle to be between the two sides. Here, ∠C is not between AB and BC.

questions.question: Two isosceles triangles have equal vertical angles. They are congruent if:

options.C: One leg of one is equal to one leg of the other

questions.explanation: Equal vertical angles + equal legs = SAS. Equal vertical angles + equal base = ASA (since base angles would also be equal).

questions.question: If △ABC ≅ △PQR, then which of the following is false?
questions.explanation: ∠A must correspond to ∠P, not ∠Q.

questions.question: In △ABC, the bisector of ∠A is also the altitude to the base BC. The triangle is:
questions.explanation: Using ASA on the two smaller triangles formed by the altitude proves AB=AC.

questions.question: Can two triangles be congruent if they have different orientations (e.g., one is rotated)?
questions.explanation: Congruence depends only on shape and size, not position or orientation.

questions.question: If △ABC ≅ △DEF by SSS, and AB=5cm, BC=6cm, AC=7cm, what is the length of DF?
questions.explanation: AC corresponds to DF in the congruent naming. So DF = AC = 7 cm.

questions.question: In a quadrilateral ABCD, if diagonal AC bisects ∠A and ∠C, then △ABC ≅ △ADC by:
questions.explanation: ∠BAC=∠DAC, AC=AC (common), and ∠BCA=∠DCA.

questions.question: Which criterion is used to show that the diagonals of a rectangle are equal?
questions.explanation: By taking two triangles using sides and the 90° angle, we use SAS.

questions.question: If △PQR is an isosceles triangle with PQ = PR, then the altitude from P to QR bisects QR because:

options.A: Triangles formed are congruent by RHS
options.B: Triangles formed are congruent by AAA
options.C: PQ is always equal to QR
options.D: It is a property of all triangles

questions.explanation: The altitude creates two right triangles with a common side and equal hypotenuses (PQ=PR).

questions.question: If the areas of two congruent triangles are 20 sq. cm each, their heights:
questions.explanation: Since the triangles are identical, their corresponding altitudes (heights) must be equal.

questions.question: Two line segments AB and CD intersect at O such that AO = OB and CO = OD. Then △AOC ≅ △BOD by:
questions.explanation: AO=BO, CO=DO, and ∠AOC = ∠BOD (Vertically Opposite Angles).

questions.question: The ASA criterion is the same as the AAS criterion because:

options.B: The third angle is automatically determined by the other two
options.D: AAS is only for right triangles

questions.explanation: Due to Angle Sum Property, if two angles are equal, the third must be equal, allowing ASA to be applied.

questions.question: If △ABC ≅ △PQR, then ∠B + ∠C is equal to:
questions.explanation: ∠B=∠Q and ∠C=∠R, so their sums are equal.

questions.question: To prove △ABC ≅ △PQR by SSS, if AB=PQ and BC=QR, what else is needed?
questions.explanation: SSS requires the third side (AC and PR) to be equal.

questions.question: Which of the following describes 'geometric twins' accurately?

options.C: Two shapes that coincide when supervised
options.D: Two shapes with same number of sides

questions.explanation: Congruent figures (geometric twins) perfectly cover each other when placed one over another.
